title: Weighted Voting
concept: In boosting, the final prediction is a weighted vote where each base model's vote strength is determined by its accuracy during training — highly accurate models get louder votes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What happens to a misclassified row's weight in AdaBoost?

options.text: It increases, making it a high priority for the next model.
It decreases, allowing the model to focus on easy rows.

exercises.explain: Correct! Error rows get weight increases.

cards.title: The unequal committee
cards.body: If you ask a panel of 3 people for weather advice — a professional meteorologist, a farmer who tracks seasons, and a random passerby — do you give their opinions equal weight? Probably not. You trust the meteorologist's prediction the most. In boosting ensembles, we calculate a mathematical weight for each model based on how accurate it was.

cards.title: Weighted Voting: Model influence
cards.body: In bagging, all trees get an equal vote. In boosting, we use **weighted voting**. Each base model is assigned an influence score (often called **Amount of Say** or **alpha**). Stumps with high accuracy get high alpha scores (loud votes); stumps that perform near random guess levels get alpha scores near zero.

cards.title: Megaphones for experts
cards.body: Think of alpha weights like megaphones. An expert model gets a large, loud megaphone. A model that is barely better than a coin flip gets a tiny, quiet megaphone. When it's time to vote, the loud megaphone easily drowns out the quiet ones.

cards.title: Resolving a split vote
cards.body: An ensemble has 3 stumps predicting if a customer will buy a product. Stump 1 (accuracy 90%, alpha = **2.0**) votes Yes. Stump 2 (accuracy 55%, alpha = **0.2**) votes No. Stump 3 (accuracy 52%, alpha = **0.1**) votes No. Although there are two 'No' votes vs one 'Yes', the weighted sum is: Yes (2.0) vs No (0.2 + 0.1 = 0.3). The final prediction is Yes.

cards.title: Even terrible models can help
cards.body: You might assume a model with an accuracy of 10% is useless and gets an alpha of 0. In binary classification, a model that is wrong 90% of the time is actually highly useful — you can just flip its prediction to be right 90% of the time! Boosting algorithms give high alpha scores to both highly accurate *and* highly inaccurate models (with a negative sign to flip their vote).

cards.title: Weighted voting vs. Equal voting
cards.body: Comparing voting mechanics in bagging vs. boosting:

left.text:
All trees have the same say.
Vote count: 1 tree = 1 vote.
Simple majority wins.

right.text:
Model say depends on training accuracy.
Vote count: Model vote * alpha weight.
Highest weighted sum wins.

exercises.prompt: How is a base model's voting power (Amount of Say / alpha) determined in boosting?

options.text: It is calculated based on the model's prediction accuracy during training.
It is assigned randomly by the user.
All models are automatically given equal say.
It is determined by the size of the validation set.

exercises.explain: Correct! The more accurate the base learner during training, the higher its voting weight (alpha).

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a boosting weighted vote, a single model with a high accuracy weight can outvote multiple weaker models combined.
exercises.explain: True! A model with a large alpha (e.g. 2.0) carries more influence than several models with low alpha values (e.g. 0.1) combined.

exercises.sentence: If a base learner has an accuracy rate of exactly 50% (no better than a coin flip), its alpha voting weight will be close to ___.
exercises.explain: A model that performs at coin-flip levels adds no information, so its voting power drops to zero.

exercises.prompt: Which prediction is the result of a weighted vote?

options.text: Classify as 'Spam' because the weighted vote sum is Spam: 2.4, Inbox: 0.8.
Classify as 'Spam' because 3 out of 4 trees predicted Spam.
Classify as 'Spam' because it was the default value.

exercises.explain: Correct! Resolving a vote based on the sum of model weights (alpha) is weighted voting.

exercises.prompt: An AdaBoost ensemble classifies a medical scan. Stump A (alpha = 1.8) predicts 'Healthy'. Stump B (alpha = 0.8) and Stump C (alpha = 0.7) predict 'Risk'. What is the final ensemble classification, and why?

options.text: 'Healthy', because the voting weight of Stump A (1.8) is greater than the combined weight of Stump B and C (0.8 + 0.7 = 1.5).
'Risk', because two stumps voted Risk vs. one voting Healthy.
A tie, because the number of votes is split.
Healthy, because Stump A was trained first.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Weighted voting sum: Healthy = 1.8; Risk = 0.8 + 0.7 = 1.5. Since Healthy has the higher weighted score, it wins the vote, demonstrating how a strong model can outvote multiple weaker ones. Next, let's compare bagging and boosting side-by-side!
